metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the explicit `jq -e` command for the `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` sub-claim in AC1, using the correct path in `terraform show -json` plan output (e.g., `.configuration.root_module.resources[] | select(.address == "aws_ssm_parameter.opcert_ed25519_key") | .expressions.lifecycle.ignore_changes | any(. == ["value"])`)
findings.finding: The plan claims 'EVERY AC1 sub-claim is asserted the same structured way — a `jq -e` query per sub-claim' and specifically names `.configuration.root_module` as the path for the `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` assertion on the Ed25519 key parameter, but provides no actual jq command for this sub-claim. In `terraform show -json` plan output, `lifecycle.ignore_changes` is represented under `.configuration.root_module.resources[].expressions` in a non-obvious structure (the key may appear as `"ignore_changes"` with an opaque internal reference array, not the literal string `"value"`), making the concrete proving command non-obvious to an executor.
findings.location: AC1 — lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] } sub-claim assertion
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC1: `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '[.configuration.root_module.resources[] | select(.address | test("aws_ssm_parameter.*opcert_ed25519_key"))] | length == 1 and .[0].expressions.lifecycle.ignore_changes != null'` — adjust the address pattern to match the actual Terraform resource label used in the IaC.

findings.finding: AC1 provides a concrete jq proving command only for the IAM invoke-policy sub-claim; the remaining AC1 assertions — (a) https:// integration URI, (b) X-Opcert-Guard static request-header mapping on the integration, (c) /rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key SecureString with lifecycle ignore_changes, (d) /rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard SecureString under the existing path grant, (e) hashicorp/random in required_providers — are covered only by the vague phrase 'A test asserts these declarations exist' with no stated command or query.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 (second and third sub-bullets after the jq block)
findings.suggested_fix:
Extend the AC1 test block with additional `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e` assertions, e.g.:
• URI: `... | select(.type=="aws_apigatewayv2_integration") | .values.integration_uri | startswith("https://")`
• Guard header: `... | select(.type=="aws_apigatewayv2_integration") | .values.request_parameters["overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard"]`
• Key lifecycle: `... | select(.type=="aws_ssm_parameter" and .values.name=="/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key") | .values.lifecycle.ignore_changes | contains(["value"])`
• Random provider: `grep -E '"random"' infra/terraform/.terraform.lock.hcl` (post-init) or check versions.tf directly.

findings.finding: The private Ed25519 key generation and SSM seeding is an explicitly irreversible operation ("the key exists nowhere else"), but the plan provides no documented recovery procedure for accidental SSM parameter deletion or Terraform state loss — the trust anchor would be permanently broken with no back-out path.
findings.location: ## What — SSM Parameter Store / Deploy + prove section; AC bullet 1 (lifecycle { ignore_changes })
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/runbooks/opcert-key-recovery.md` (or a section in `infra/terraform/README.md`) documenting: (1) detect: service returns signing errors / public key mismatch; (2) recover: `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N "" -f /tmp/opcert-ed25519`; `aws ssm put-parameter --name /rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key --value "$(cat /tmp/opcert-ed25519)" --type SecureString --overwrite`; shred local copy; (3) re-pin: update `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` with new public key and new `added_at_log_position`. Reference this runbook from the AC operator-attestation comment template.

findings.finding: The ticket's `file_impact` structured metadata lists `infra/opcert/` as the Terraform edit path (with reason 'Terraform for API GW + Fargate + Secrets Manager'), but this path does not exist in the repo and the plan body explicitly places all new Terraform in the existing `infra/terraform/` directory. The stale reason also contradicts the plan's explicit rejection of Fargate and Secrets Manager.
findings.location: Ticket `file_impact` field / What section (Terraform)
findings.suggested_fix: Change file_impact entry to `{"path": "infra/terraform/", "reason": "Extend with API Gateway HTTP API v2, IAM role rebar-opcert-admin, SSM SecureString parameters, hashicorp/random provider"}`.

findings.finding: AC1 asserts that 'EVERY AC1 sub-claim is asserted the same structured way — a `jq -e` query per sub-claim against the one `terraform show -json tf.plan` output' for at least five distinct sub-claims (the `https://` integration URI scheme, the `X-Opcert-Guard` static request-parameter mapping, the two SecureString parameters and their names, the `AWS_IAM` route authorization, and the key parameter's `lifecycle.ignore_changes` block), but only the Invoke-policy assertion command is written out in full. An executing agent must derive the remaining jq commands independently, with no guarantee they use the correct JSON key paths or pass the criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, second paragraph beginning 'EVERY AC1 sub-claim is asserted the same structured way'
findings.suggested_fix:
Add the following concrete commands to AC1 (verify exact provider key names against `terraform show -json` output on a test plan before committing):
```bash
# https:// integration URI
terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '
  [.planned_values.root_module.resources[]
   | select(.type == "aws_apigatewayv2_integration")
   | select(.values.integration_uri | startswith("https://"))]
  | length >= 1'

# X-Opcert-Guard static request parameter
terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '
  [.planned_values.root_module.resources[]
   | select(.type == "aws_apigatewayv2_integration")
   | .values.request_parameters
   | to_entries[]
   | select(.key | test("X-Opcert-Guard"; "i"))]
  | length == 1'

# Both SecureString parameters with correct names
terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '
  [.planned_values.root_module.resources[]
   | select(.type == "aws_ssm_parameter" and .values.type == "SecureString")
   | .values.name]
  | (contains(["/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key"]) and contains(["/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard"]))'

# AWS_IAM route authorization_type
terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '
  [.planned_values.root_module.resources[]
   | select(.type == "aws_apigatewayv2_route")
   | select(.values.authorization_type == "AWS_IAM")]
  | length >= 1'

# lifecycle ignore_changes = ["value"] on the key parameter
terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '
  [.configuration.root_module.resources[]
   | select(.type == "aws_ssm_parameter")
   | select(.expressions.name.constant_value == "/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key")
   | select(.lifecycle.ignore_changes | contains(["value"]))]
  | length == 1'
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the leaf ticket's `file_impact` metadata to replace `{"path": "infra/opcert/", "reason": "Terraform for API GW + Fargate + Secrets Manager"}` with the correct paths `infra/terraform/` and `infra/compose/` (and `infra/nginx/` + `infra/scripts/`) consistent with the parent's settled runtime decision and the leaf's own plan text.
findings.finding: The leaf ticket's file_impact metadata lists path 'infra/opcert/' with reason 'Terraform for API GW + Fargate + Secrets Manager' — a pre-cost-verification artifact that contradicts the parent's settled decision (Fargate explicitly rejected; implementation files belong in infra/terraform/ and infra/compose/, not infra/opcert/) and the leaf's own plan text.
findings.location: Leaf ticket file_impact field: {"path": "infra/opcert/", "reason": "Terraform for API GW + Fargate + Secrets Manager"}
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the file_impact entry with: [{"path": "infra/terraform/", "reason": "API Gateway HTTP API v2 + IAM role + SSM SecureString parameters + random provider"}, {"path": "infra/compose/", "reason": "opcert service in docker-compose.yml + Dockerfile.opcert (sibling dep)"}, {"path": "infra/nginx/", "reason": "rebar.conf.template gains /opcert/ location + opcert-guard.conf include"}, {"path": "infra/scripts/", "reason": "materialize-opcert-guard.sh + compose-up.sh wiring"}, {"path": ".rebar/trusted_environments.yaml", "reason": "pin deployed env public key + env_id"}]

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the AC1 jq assertion for `execute-api:Invoke` to also verify `.s.Resource` contains the expected API execution ARN pattern (e.g. `arn:aws:execute-api:*`/`*`/`*/opcert/*` scoped to this API), so a `Resource: "*"` policy fails the test.
findings.finding: The AC1 structured jq assertion verifies that exactly one `execute-api:Invoke`-granting resource exists and that its Terraform label is `opcert_admin_invoke`, but it does NOT inspect the `Resource` field of the policy statement — a policy with `"Resource": "*"` (invoke on any API) would pass the assertion unchanged, silently violating the plan's own least-privilege claim ('scoped to exactly this API's execution ARN').
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1 — jq assertion for execute-api:Invoke
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the existing jq pipeline: `and (.[0].s.Resource | if type == "array" then . else [.] end | all(test("arn:aws:execute-api:[^:]+:[^:]+:<API_ID>/")))`; or parameterise with the Terraform output `aws_apigatewayv2_api.opcert.id` substituted at test time.

findings.finding: The AC1 structured jq -e assertion verifies only the permission policy (that exactly one execute-api:Invoke grant exists with label 'opcert_admin_invoke') but does NOT verify the rebar-opcert-admin role's trust policy is actually restricted to the principal ARNs in opcert_admin_principal_arns. A misconfigured or overly-broad trust policy (e.g., trusting 'Principal: *' or the entire AWS account) would allow any authenticated AWS identity to assume the role and call Invoke — the boundary-crossing sensitive path has an undeclared/unverified access posture on the trust-policy dimension.
findings.location: AC1 — structured jq assertion / 'What' section: 'trust policy limited to the principal ARNs supplied via a new variable opcert_admin_principal_arns'
findings.suggested_fix: In the variable declaration: add `validation { condition = length(var.opcert_admin_principal_arns) > 0 && !contains(var.opcert_admin_principal_arns, '*') error_message = 'opcert_admin_principal_arns must be a non-empty list of specific IAM principal ARNs.' }`. In AC1: add a jq sub-claim such as `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] | select(.type=="aws_iam_role" and .name=="rebar_opcert_admin")] | .[0].values.assume_role_policy | fromjson | .Statement[] | select(.Principal | (type=="object" and (.AWS=="*" or .Federated=="*")) or .=="*") | error' 2>/dev/null; [ $? -ne 0 ]`

findings.finding: The `rebar-opcert-admin` IAM role's trust policy (restricting which principals may assume the role via `opcert_admin_principal_arns`) is declared as a security property in AC1 but has no structured, self-failing assertion — the only machine-verifiable jq command in AC1 validates the *permission policy* (that exactly one `execute-api:Invoke` grant exists on the named resource), not the trust policy. An IaC defect that set the trust policy to `"*"` or `"arn:aws:iam::*:root"` would pass the AC1 test command while granting any AWS principal the ability to assume the admin role and invoke the opcert endpoint.
findings.location: AC1 — structured jq assertion; What section — 'trust policy limited to the principal ARNs supplied via a new variable opcert_admin_principal_arns'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC1 jq command (or add a second command) such as: `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] | select(.type == "aws_iam_role" and .name == "rebar_opcert_admin")] | length == 1 and (.[0].values.assume_role_policy | fromjson | .Statement[] | .Principal | to_entries | all(.value != "*"))'`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }` to the `aws_ssm_parameter` resource for `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` (the sole copy of the private key) to match the `prevent_destroy` pattern already applied to the EBS data volume and Elastic IP in `main.tf`.
findings.finding: The SSM SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` is explicitly described as the sole copy of the Ed25519 private key ('the key exists nowhere else'), and `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` correctly prevents value overwrite — but the plan does not specify `prevent_destroy = true` on this parameter. A `terraform destroy` or an accidental `terraform state rm` + re-apply would silently delete the only copy of the key, an irreversible loss. The pattern is already established in this codebase: `aws_ebs_volume.data` and `aws_eip.gerrit` both carry `prevent_destroy` in `main.tf`.
findings.location: What section — SSM Parameter Store provisioning; AC1 (lifecycle block spec); compare infra/terraform/main.tf lines 32-34 and 94-96
findings.suggested_fix:
In the new SSM parameter resource for the Ed25519 key, combine both lifecycle directives:
```hcl
lifecycle {
  ignore_changes  = [value]
  prevent_destroy = true
}
```
And add a structured AC assertion: `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '[.configuration.root_module.resources[] | select(.address == "aws_ssm_parameter.opcert_ed25519_key") | .expressions.lifecycle.prevent_destroy.constant_value] | .[0] == true'`.

findings.finding: The SSM SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` is explicitly described as the sole copy of the key ('the key exists nowhere else') yet the plan does not declare `prevent_destroy` on it. A `terraform destroy -target` on that parameter — or a state rm + apply — silently and irreversibly destroys the only copy, with no Terraform safeguard.
findings.location: What section — SSM Parameter Store SecureString; AC1: 'the two SecureString parameters (the key parameter guarded by lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] })'
findings.suggested_fix:
In the new `aws_ssm_parameter` resource for `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`, use:
```hcl
lifecycle {
  ignore_changes  = [value]
  prevent_destroy = true
}
```
This matches the existing precedent on `aws_ebs_volume.data` for irreplaceable stateful resources.

findings.finding: The SSM SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` — explicitly described as the only copy of the Ed25519 private key — has `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` to prevent Terraform from overwriting the operator-seeded key, but the plan specifies NO `prevent_destroy` on this parameter. A `terraform destroy`, a state-level `terraform state rm` followed by re-apply, or accidental module deletion would destroy the SSM parameter and permanently lose the key with no recovery path.
findings.location: What section — SSM Parameter Store SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`; AC1 — 'the key parameter guarded by lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }'

findings.finding: The plan asserts that `terraform show -json tf.plan` exposes `lifecycle.ignore_changes` under `.configuration.root_module` and that a `jq -e` query against that path can structurally verify the key parameter's `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` block. However, in Terraform's JSON plan output, `lifecycle` metadata including `ignore_changes` appears under `.configuration.root_module.resources[].lifecycle.ignore_changes` as an array of attribute-name strings — but only when the resource uses a `for_each` iterator does it vary; for a single resource the path is `.configuration.root_module.resources[] | select(.address == "aws_ssm_parameter.opcert_key") | .lifecycle.ignore_changes`. The plan does not specify the exact jq path or the expected array value (e.g. `["value"]`), leaving the assertion underspecified and potentially failing if the path is wrong.
findings.location: AC1 — 'lifecycle.ignore_changes block … `.configuration.root_module` for the key parameter's `lifecycle.ignore_changes` block'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exact Terraform attribute and key notation for static header injection on `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` (e.g. `request_parameters = { "overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard" = "$context.requestId" }` style notation with the actual value expression), so the implementer can verify it against provider docs before writing HCL.
findings.finding: The plan describes injecting the `X-Opcert-Guard` value as a 'static request-header parameter' on the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource but never names the specific Terraform attribute (`request_parameters`) or its required key notation (e.g. `"overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard"`). For HTTP_PROXY integrations, `request_parameters` uses a specific overwrite/append/custom key syntax that differs from REST API mappings; if an implementer uses the wrong attribute name or notation, `terraform validate` will catch the name error but a wrong notation string silently produces no header injection at runtime. This surface is Partially-verified.
findings.location: What section — API Gateway HTTP API paragraph: 'injecting a shared origin-guard header' and AC1: 'the X-Opcert-Guard static request-parameter mapping'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'the static header is declared via `request_parameters = { "overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard" = "<guard-value-expression>" }` on the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource — verify the exact overwrite/append/custom prefix against the hashicorp/aws provider docs for HTTP_PROXY integrations before coding.'

findings.finding: The plan's boot-ordering section states the nginx-config install step 'ALSO installs a DENY-ALL default /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf' but the step that installs rebar.conf from the template to /etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf is not owned by any existing script in the repo (install-certbot-timer.sh references it only in a comment; compose-up.sh does not install it). The plan piggybacks on this 'nginx-config install step' without naming the script/runbook step that owns it or declaring it as an edit target, leaving the deny-all guard install unrooted.
findings.location: What section / Compose wiring / boot ordering hazard (a)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the claim 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' — HTTP API v2 does support resource-based policies via the `policy` argument on `aws_apigatewayv2_api`; restate the design decision as a deliberate choice (identity policy is sufficient for this use case) rather than a capability absence.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' as the architectural rationale for relying solely on identity-based IAM policy — but AWS API Gateway HTTP APIs (v2) DO support resource-based policies (the `aws_apigatewayv2_api` Terraform resource accepts a `policy` argument). This claim is factually Contradicted by the AWS provider, and if an implementer accepts it at face value they will believe a defense-in-depth resource policy is architecturally impossible when it is not.
findings.location: What section — API Gateway HTTP API paragraph: 'an HTTP API has no resource policy'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' with: 'identity-policy-only is sufficient here; a resource policy on the HTTP API is an option but is not added — the admin role's inline invoke grant combined with SigV4 auth on each route is the access-control boundary.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a shell-history-safe SSM seeding invocation in the 'Deploy + prove' runbook (e.g. `aws ssm put-parameter --value file://<(cat /path/to/privkey)` or redirecting the value from a temp file with `history -d` / `HISTFILE=/dev/null`) to honour the 'key exists nowhere else' security property.
findings.finding: The 'Deploy + prove' step (AC5) generates the Ed25519 private key with `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N ""` and seeds it via `aws ssm put-parameter`; the plan does not specify a shell-history-safe invocation pattern. If the operator passes the key via `--value "$(cat privkey)"` or inline, the plaintext private key appears in `.bash_history` and in `/proc` process listings for the duration of the call — on the operator's machine this is low blast-radius, but the plan's own stated security property is 'the key exists nowhere else' (including logs).
findings.location: What section — 'Deploy + prove' step; AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Document: `HISTFILE=/dev/null aws ssm put-parameter --name /rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key --type SecureString --value "$(cat /tmp/opcert_ed25519)" --overwrite && shred -u /tmp/opcert_ed25519` — or use `--cli-input-json` with a temp file that is shredded after use.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC3 sub-assertion that verifies the nginx config install recipe (the `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` rendering step) writes a deny-all `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` before `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` is invoked — e.g., assert the install script/template contains the literal `map $http_x_opcert_guard $opcert_guard_ok { default 0; }` default-deny block.
findings.finding: The deny-all default `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` installation — described in What as a critical boot-ordering hazard closure, explicitly added to the nginx config install recipe — has no covering acceptance criterion. All ACs could pass while an implementer skips this step, leaving the boot ordering hazard open (nginx fails to start or serves unguarded /opcert/ on a fresh boot before materialize runs).
findings.location: What (boot hazard paragraph) vs AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3 with: 'and that the nginx config install step writes a deny-all `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` (`map $http_x_opcert_guard $opcert_guard_ok { default 0; }`) so the box is fail-closed on cold boot before `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` has run.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the `https://` integration URI scheme assertion in AC3 is intentionally redundant with AC1's jq sub-claim (belt-and-suspenders for a security-relevant property) or accidental — if intentional, add a parenthetical note in AC3 explaining the duplication is deliberate; if accidental, remove it from AC3.
findings.finding: The `https://` integration URI scheme requirement appears as an asserted sub-claim in both AC1 (via `jq -e` against `terraform show -json tf.plan`) and AC3 (via unit test asserting 'the Terraform integration URI uses the `https://` scheme'), creating a near-duplicate assertion split across two criteria.
findings.location: AC1 (jq sub-claims list) and AC3 (final sentence)
findings.suggested_fix: Add '(belt-and-suspenders with AC1's structured plan assertion)' after the AC3 mention, or remove it from AC3 and note it is fully covered by AC1's jq sub-claim.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out procedure for the nginx config change: e.g., keep the current rendered config as `/etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf.bak` before applying the new template, with a one-liner to restore it and reload nginx if validation fails (`nginx -t || cp /etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf.bak /etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf && nginx -s reload`).
findings.finding: The plan modifies the production host-nginx config (`infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template`) that currently serves the Gerrit instance — the only box in rebar's AWS footprint — but provides no rollback/back-out plan. If the extended template fails to render, contains a syntax error, or causes nginx to reject the reload, Gerrit goes down with no documented recovery procedure. T4 requires an explicit rollback mechanism, not merely acknowledgement of the change.
findings.location: What section — nginx config changes: 'infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template — which today has NO /opcert/ location or include — gains both the include /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf; directive and a location /opcert/ { … } block'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' nginx section and in AC3, add: 'Before rendering the new template, the install recipe copies the current `/etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf` to `/etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf.bak`. After rendering, it runs `nginx -t`; on failure it restores the backup and exits non-zero. The rollback one-liner is: `cp /etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf.bak /etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf && nginx -s reload`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate a specific checked-in artifact (e.g., a header comment block in `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` itself, or a `docs/runbooks/opcert-guard-rotation.md`) that documents the two-step rotation procedure and the fail-closed window, and add an AC assertion that the file exists and contains the key rotation commands.
findings.finding: The guard-rotation runbook (two ordered steps: `terraform apply -replace=random_password.opcert_guard` then re-run `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` + `nginx -s reload`) is specified only in the story description and has no designated checked-in artifact. After the story closes, operators cannot discover the rotation procedure from the repository itself.
findings.location: What section — 'ROTATION — the documented runbook is two steps in order: terraform apply -replace=random_password.opcert_guard … then re-run materialize-opcert-guard.sh'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Terraform IaC and AC1: an `aws_apigatewayv2_stage` with `access_log_settings` pointing to a CloudWatch log group (or confirm the existing stage captures access logs), and at minimum a CloudWatch metric filter + alarm on 4xx/5xx rates for the new API.
findings.finding: No observability (CloudWatch access logs, metrics, or alarms) is specified for the new API Gateway endpoint or the opcert compose service. The plan introduces a new externally-reachable endpoint whose failure modes (5xx from the origin, repeated 403s from missing guard header, SigV4 auth failures) would be invisible to operators.
findings.location: ## What — API Gateway / Terraform section; ## Acceptance Criteria AC5
findings.suggested_fix: In the Terraform section add: `aws_cloudwatch_log_group.opcert_apigw_access_logs` + `access_log_settings { destination_arn = … format = … }` on the stage; add an AC sub-claim asserting the log group exists in the plan output.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` retries the SSM read with backoff (e.g. up to 3 attempts, 5 s apart) and exits non-zero with a logged error on final failure — so `compose-up.sh` can surface the failure and systemd/the operator is notified rather than the endpoint silently staying deny-all.
findings.finding: The `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` script's SSM read failure at boot is not addressed with any retry or operator-surfacing mechanism. While the fail-closed default (DENY-ALL nginx config) is correctly specified, a transient SSM unavailability (cold-boot IAM credential propagation lag, brief network partition) would leave /opcert/ permanently deny-all with no log entry, alert, or automatic retry — the operator would only notice when the endpoint stays broken.
findings.location: ## What — Compose / nginx wiring, ordering hazard (a) BOOT
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the script spec: a retry loop (`for i in 1 2 3; do aws ssm get-parameter … && break || sleep 5; done || { echo 'FATAL: SSM read failed' >&2; exit 1; }`) and document that compose-up.sh propagates a non-zero exit to its caller/systemd.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a key-rotation runbook (at minimum: `aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite` to seed a new key, update `trusted_environments.yaml` with the new public key and a new `added_at_log_position`, restart the compose service) and a decommission step (delete both SSM parameters, remove the `trusted_environments.yaml` entry) to close the UPDATE and RETIRE lifecycle gaps for `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`.
findings.finding: The Ed25519 private key's rotation and retirement lifecycle is unspecified. The plan seeds the key out-of-band and guards it with `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` so Terraform never touches it again, but provides no procedure for key compromise, planned rotation, or service decommission — leaving the most sensitive shared state with no UPDATE or RETIRE path.
findings.location: What section — SSM Parameter Store SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`; AC1 (key parameter sub-claim)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to the deploy runbook: **Key rotation**: (1) generate new key locally, (2) `aws ssm put-parameter --name /rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key --value "$(cat new_key)" --type SecureString --overwrite`, (3) restart opcert compose service, (4) update `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` with new public key + new `added_at_log_position`. **Decommission**: delete both `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` and `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` SSM params, run `terraform destroy` on opcert resources, remove the entry from `trusted_environments.yaml`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the precedent-location conflict: either move `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` to `infra/gerrit/` to match the cited precedent (`materialize-deploy-key.sh`, `materialize-g2p-config.sh`), or explicitly acknowledge the divergence and explain why `infra/scripts/` is correct for this case.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` script follows the 'infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh / materialize-g2p-config.sh — SSM → file' precedent, but then places the new script at `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh`. Reading the filesystem confirms that BOTH cited precedent scripts live in `infra/gerrit/`, not `infra/scripts/`. The plan's invoke-before-compose-up wiring also calls the new script from `compose-up.sh` — but `compose-up.sh` calls `infra/gerrit/materialize-g2p-config.sh` (an infra/gerrit/ path), not infra/scripts/. Placing the new script in infra/scripts/ breaks the pattern the plan itself claims as precedent and leaves the 'where does this kind of script live' convention ambiguous.
findings.location: What section — Compose paragraph; AC3 ('per the infra/gerrit/materialize-*.sh precedent')
findings.suggested_fix: Move the script to `infra/gerrit/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` and update the compose-up.sh call site accordingly — consistent with every existing materialize script.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` to the edit-target list and specify whether `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` should be added to `BOT_PATHS` (or a separate `OPCERT_PATHS` trigger) so the opcert service is rebuilt when its Dockerfile changes on main.
findings.finding: infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh is an unenumerated consumer: its BOT_PATHS variable (line 55) lists only 'src/rebar/ infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot pyproject.toml infra/compose/docker-compose.yml' as rebuild triggers, but the plan adds infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert and an opcert service to docker-compose.yml. The Dockerfile.opcert path is absent from BOT_PATHS, so autodeploy will never rebuild the opcert container when its Dockerfile changes on main.
findings.location: infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh line 55 / What section (Compose)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` to BOT_PATHS in autodeploy.sh (and handle `opcert` service rebuild analogously to the review-bot service rebuild), or explicitly document that opcert is operator-deployed only (out of scope for autodeploy) and note that in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a citation to the AWS HTTP API v2 documentation (or a comment in the Terraform source) confirming that `aws_apigatewayv2_api` (HTTP API) does not support resource-based policies, substantiating the identity-policy-only caller-restriction design.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' as the security basis for relying solely on identity policy (the IaC-created role's inline policy) to restrict execute-api:Invoke — but this is stated as bare fact with no citation to AWS documentation. HTTP API v2 behaves differently from REST API (v1) in this regard, making it a non-obvious, security-load-bearing, unverified capability claim.
findings.location: What section: 'an HTTP API has no resource policy: `AWS_IAM` auth requires SigV4, and the IaC CREATES the admin role it restricts to'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a comment in the plan (and in the Terraform source) citing https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/http-api-access-control.html, which documents that HTTP APIs support IAM authorization but do not support resource policies (unlike REST APIs). This converts the bare assertion to a cited, verifiable claim.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add API Gateway access logging (an `aws_cloudwatch_log_group` + `access_log_settings` on the HTTP API default stage) and at least one CloudWatch alarm (e.g., 5xx error rate or `IntegrationError` count) wired to the existing `aws_sns_topic.alerts`, consistent with the `monitoring_*.tf` pattern for every other component.
findings.finding: The plan provisions a new internet-reachable API Gateway HTTP API (the first new AWS service endpoint in this stack) but specifies no access logging and no CloudWatch alarm for it. Every other service component in the stack has a corresponding `monitoring_*.tf` file wired to the SNS alerts topic (gate-down, EC2 status checks, disk pressure, replication errors, voter errors, autodeploy errors, mirror sync). The new API GW stage has no `access_log_settings` block (requires an explicit `aws_cloudwatch_log_group` + stage log config in API GW v2), and no alarm on 5xx errors or failed SigV4 authentication attempts (4xx) is mentioned, leaving the new trust-anchor endpoint dark.
findings.location: What section — API Gateway HTTP API (v2) declaration; Observability & Ownership criterion; compare infra/terraform/monitoring*.tf pattern

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State in the plan the format and generation procedure for `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` (e.g., 'a UUID v4 generated once at deploy with `python3 -c "import uuid; print(uuid.uuid4())"`  and hardcoded in docker-compose.yml and trusted_environments.yaml'), or quote the relevant excerpt from the 'Authoritative era anchor' resolution directly.
findings.finding: The plan wires `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` as an environment variable in the compose service and requires it to match the `env_id` entry in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` (AC4), but nowhere specifies the format, generation mechanism, or value of this identifier — whether it is a UUID generated at deploy time, a hostname, a short human-chosen label, or another form. An executor writing the compose file and the YAML cannot determine what value to use without external context.
findings.location: What § (Compose section) and AC4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a new ADR (or a `docs/` design note linked from CLAUDE.md) capturing the HTTP API v2 + AWS_IAM + host-nginx origin-guard pattern, its threat model, and why VPC Link / Lambda authorizer / dedicated Fargate were rejected — declare the ADR file as an explicit deliverable in ## Scope.
findings.finding: No new ADR (or equivalent design-doc update) is declared as a deliverable for the novel API Gateway HTTP API v2 + AWS_IAM + host-nginx origin-guard architectural pattern. The plan references ADR 0046/0047 and an epic-level 'design resolution' for cost and runtime decisions, but the specific combination — HTTP API SigV4 auth with a static request-header origin guard forwarded to a host-nginx TLS origin — is itself a novel integration pattern not captured in any named, versioned artifact this story produces.
findings.location: ## What — Terraform section; ## Why paragraph 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add `infra/docs/adr-NNNN-opcert-gateway-pattern.md` to ## Scope (In) and add an AC line: 'An ADR capturing the API Gateway SigV4 + origin-guard design and the alternatives considered is merged alongside the IaC.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `nginx -t` before every `nginx -s reload` call (in both `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` and the boot orchestrator) and document a rollback procedure (e.g. restore the prior rendered config from a backup copy and reload) as a named step in ## Scope or a linked runbook.
findings.finding: No rollback procedure is defined for the nginx config template change (`infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template`). The plan adds an `include` directive and a new `location /opcert/` block to a template that currently serves all traffic (including Gerrit). A syntax error or misconfiguration in these additions could cause `nginx -s reload` (or the initial template render) to fail, taking down all nginx-served routes — including Gerrit — with no documented recovery path.
findings.location: ## What — Compose / nginx wiring section; ## Acceptance Criteria AC3
findings.suggested_fix: In the materialize script: `nginx -t && nginx -s reload || { echo 'nginx config test failed, aborting reload' >&2; exit 1; }`. In the boot recipe: make a backup of the rendered config before overwriting (`cp /etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf.bak`) so rollback is `cp … .bak … && nginx -s reload`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` writes the guard conf atomically (e.g., write to `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf.tmp` then `mv -f` to `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf`) before calling `nginx -s reload`, so a mid-write crash leaves the previous valid conf intact.
findings.finding: The `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` script writes `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` with no mention of an atomic write (write-to-tempfile + rename). A crash or SIGKILL mid-write could leave a partially-written file, causing `nginx -s reload` to fail with a parse error and leaving the old (or no) guard value active.
findings.location: What section — compose/nginx wiring; `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` (declared target)
findings.suggested_fix: In the materialize script spec, replace direct write with: `printf 'map $http_x_opcert_guard $opcert_guard_ok { default 0; "%s" 1; }\n' "$GUARD_VALUE" > /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf.tmp && mv -f /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf.tmp /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf && nginx -s reload`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the RETIRE path for the `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` entry: specify that decommissioning the opcert service requires removing (or marking deprecated) the entry and recording the final log position at which the entry is considered expired.
findings.finding: The `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` pinned entry has no defined retirement/cleanup path. The plan specifies adding the entry (with `added_at_log_position`) but does not specify how or when a stale entry is removed if the environment is decommissioned, the key is rotated, or the service is retired.
findings.location: What section — Deploy + prove; AC4 (trusted_environments.yaml)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `hashicorp/random` / `random_password` is justified over extending the existing operator-seed + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` SSM pattern (already used for all 12+ existing secrets in `ssm.tf`) — if the Terraform-managed guard rotation is genuinely required, document why the existing pattern cannot serve; otherwise consider generating the guard value via `openssl rand` and seeding it out-of-band like every other SSM secret.
findings.finding: `hashicorp/random` (`random_password`) is introduced as a new Terraform provider with zero existing call-sites in `infra/terraform/` (Rule-of-Three: 0/3), while the codebase's established pattern for all 12+ SSM secret values is the operator-seed + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` model in `ssm.tf`. The plan adds a new provider and a new Terraform-managed rotation lifecycle (`terraform apply -replace=random_password.opcert_guard`) solely for this one value — NIH relative to the pattern already proven at scale in the repo.
findings.location: What section — 'the origin-guard header provisioning path: Terraform generates the guard value with `random_password` … the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to `infra/terraform/versions.tf`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (or state explicitly) which story creates `trusted_environments.yaml` and establishes its schema (including the `added_at_log_position` field); if it's a dependency, name the ticket; if this story creates it, declare that in Scope.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` exists (or that its schema with `added_at_log_position` is already established by the epic's 'Authoritative era anchor' resolution) as the target for pinning the deployed environment's public key (AC4). A filesystem search finds no `trusted_environments.yaml` anywhere in the repo (only references in `.rebar/cache/` review artefacts). If this file does not yet exist on `main`, AC4's test assertion — 'a test asserts the entry parses and the `env_id` matches' — has no pre-existing artifact to parse against a schema, and the schema itself (including the `added_at_log_position` field) is unverifiable from the repo.
findings.location: AC4; Deploy + prove section ('pin the environment's public key + env_id into .rebar/trusted_environments.yaml per the epic's Authoritative era anchor resolution')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly identify who creates `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` and defines its schema (this story, the sibling story, or the epic's 'Authoritative era anchor' resolution), and either add the file/schema as an in-scope deliverable of this plan or add it to the sibling story's declared deliverables.
findings.finding: .rebar/trusted_environments.yaml does not exist in the repository and is not named as a deliverable of the depends_on sibling story (which is stated to supply only src/rebar/opcert_service/ and infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert). AC4 instructs pinning into this file and asserts a test that 'the entry parses', treating the file and its schema as pre-established, but neither the file nor its defining schema appears anywhere on main or in the sibling story's stated deliverables.
findings.location: AC4 / What section ('pin the environment's public key + env_id into .rebar/trusted_environments.yaml')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief justification in the plan for why `hashicorp/random` + Terraform-managed rotation is preferred over the established operator-seeded + `lifecycle { ignore_changes }` pattern for the origin-guard value (e.g., Terraform-atomic rotation semantics vs. two-step operator seeding).
findings.finding: `hashicorp/random` (`random_password`) is introduced as a new Terraform provider dependency with zero existing call-sites in `infra/terraform/` (Rule-of-Three: 0 of 3), while the codebase's established pattern for SSM-stored secrets is operator-seeded + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`. The plan does not explain why the `random_password` approach is preferred over the existing project pattern (operator generates and seeds the guard value once, same as the signing key).
findings.location: What section: 'the origin-guard header provisioning path: Terraform generates the guard value with `random_password` — the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to `infra/terraform/versions.tf` `required_providers`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the origin-guard header as an instance of the established AWS 'CloudFront/gateway custom origin header' restriction pattern, and note any known caveats (e.g., the header value must be treated as a bearer token — constant-time comparison in nginx, not logged).
findings.finding: The origin-guard header pattern (gateway injects a shared-secret header; the EC2 origin validates it to reject direct-to-origin requests) is a direct variant of the well-documented AWS 'CloudFront custom origin header' pattern and the 'restrict ALB access' pattern. The plan adopts this mechanism without acknowledging the established prior art or explaining why the API Gateway variant is adapted rather than following the documented AWS guidance for this exact use-case.
findings.location: What section: 'an nginx route `/opcert/` → the service that rejects requests whose `X-Opcert-Guard` header does not match'

findings.finding: The plan asserts that 'lines 8-9 [of infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template] render the template via `sed`', but reading the file shows lines 7-10 document `envsubst` as the rendering tool — `sed` is never mentioned. The plan's boot-hazard closure argument ('the nginx-config install recipe … is EXTENDED with one more line that installs a DENY-ALL default') rests on this false premise about the mechanism. Furthermore, the header is illustrative documentation (a comment), not an executed script; there is no `install-nginx.sh` or equivalent script in `infra/scripts/` that actually runs the `envsubst` rendering today — so the 'recipe' the plan proposes to extend does not exist as an executable artifact.
findings.location: What section — Compose/nginx wiring paragraph: 'the nginx-config install recipe — the documented header of infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template itself (its lines 8-9 render the template via sed into /etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf; no separate script owns this today)'
findings.suggested_fix: Create `infra/scripts/install-nginx-config.sh` that runs `envsubst '${REVIEW_BOT_PORT}' < infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template > /etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf` and also writes the DENY-ALL `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf`; reference this script explicitly in the plan as the artifact to extend/create, and wire it into compose-up.sh before materialize-opcert-guard.sh.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
